description:
You are a security auditor who has just received a fresh, empty Linux workstation.  
    Your goal is to create a miniature, file-based “compliance lab” under the non-privileged
    account located at /home/user.  Follow the specification below exactly; the automatic
    grader will check everything byte-for-byte or with regular-expression matches where
    indicated.

    1.  Workspace
        • Create the directory /home/user/audit_db  
          (it must already exist before any other step can happen).

    2.  Database creation  
        • Inside /home/user/audit_db create a SQLite3 database file named company.db.  
        • Inside company.db create the two tables shown below **using exactly
          these column names, types and row order**.

          a) Table: employees  
             ┌─────────┬────────┬───────┐  
             │ id(INT) │ name   │ role  │  
             └─────────┴────────┴───────┘  

             INSERT the following rows **in the listed order**:  
             1, Alice,  admin  
             2, Bob,    manager  
             3, Charlie,staff  
             4, Diana,  staff  
             5, Evan,   manager  

          b) Table: permissions  
             ┌──────────┬───────┬──────────────┐  
             │ pid(INT) │ role  │ access_level │  
             └──────────┴───────┴──────────────┘  

             INSERT the following rows **in the listed order**:  
             1, admin,   ALL  
             2, manager, READ_WRITE  
             3, staff,   READ_ONLY  

    3.  File permission hardening  
        • Set the permission bits of /home/user/audit_db/company.db to 660
          (rw-rw----).  
        • Do NOT change ownership (you do not have root).

    4.  Parallel audit queries  
        • Simulate a multi-analyst environment by running **two read-only queries
          in parallel** (background jobs, GNU parallel, & or similar are all fine):  
            – Query #1: SELECT role, COUNT(*) FROM employees GROUP BY role;  
            – Query #2: SELECT role, COUNT(*) FROM permissions GROUP BY role;  
        • Capture the individual outputs in any temporary files you like, then
          merge the results into the single log file described next.

    5.  Produce the permission audit log  
        • Create /home/user/audit_db/permission_audit.log  
        • The file **must contain exactly five lines**, each terminated with a
          single UNIX newline (LF).  No extra blank lines, no CR characters.  
        • The exact, required structure is shown below.  
          Tokens are case-sensitive.  Replace <TIMESTAMP> with the current UTC
          date-and-time in ISO-8601 basic format “YYYY-MM-DDTHH:MM:SSZ”.  
          The <TIMESTAMP> value on all three “ROLE:” lines must be identical
          because they belong to the same report batch.

          Line-by-line specification

          1) HEADER:PERMISSION_AUDIT|VERSION:1.0
          2) ROLE:admin|RESOURCES:1|USERS:Alice|TIMESTAMP:<TIMESTAMP>
          3) ROLE:manager|RESOURCES:1|USERS:Bob,Evan|TIMESTAMP:<TIMESTAMP>
          4) ROLE:staff|RESOURCES:1|USERS:Charlie,Diana|TIMESTAMP:<TIMESTAMP>
          5) FOOTER:END_OF_REPORT

          Notes on the field values:
          • RESOURCES is always “1” because each role has exactly one permission
            entry in table permissions.  
          • USERS lists employee names for that role, in alphabetical order,
            comma-separated, no embedded spaces.  
          • The grader will allow any valid UTC timestamp that matches the regex
            “^[0-9]{4}-[0-9]{2}-[0-9]{2}T[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}Z$”.

    6.  Summary file for spot-checking  
        • Create /home/user/audit_db/audit_summary.txt with the **exact**
          three-line content shown (use the same <TIMESTAMP> adopted above):

          DB_FILE_PERMISSIONS:660
          EMPLOYEE_TOTAL:5
          SUMMARY_TIMESTAMP:<TIMESTAMP>

          – Same newline rules as before: exactly three lines, each ending in LF.

    7.  What will be graded
        • Existence of /home/user/audit_db  
        • Correct permission bits on /home/user/audit_db/company.db  
        • Presence and structure of company.db with the EXACT table/row data.  
        • presence, exact line count and precise field ordering in
          /home/user/audit_db/permission_audit.log  
        • presence and exact three lines in /home/user/audit_db/audit_summary.txt  

    8.  DO NOT place the commands you use in this description.  
        You may, and should, display console output while you work—
        that will not be graded.  Only the final artefacts and
        permissions listed here are evaluated.
truth:
660 1|Alice|admin 2|Bob|manager 3|Charlie|staff 4|Diana|staff 5|Evan|manager
1|admin|ALL 2|manager|READ_WRITE 3|staff|READ_ONLY
5 ^HEADER:PERMISSION_AUDIT\|VERSION:1\.0\n ROLE:admin\|RESOURCES:1\|USERS:Alice\|TIMESTAMP:[0-9T:Z]{20}\n ROLE:manager\|RESOURCES:1\|USERS:Bob,Evan\|TIMESTAMP:[0-9T:Z]{20}\n ROLE:staff\|RESOURCES:1\|USERS:Charlie,Diana\|TIMESTAMP:[0-9T:Z]{20}\n FOOTER:END_OF_REPORT\n?$ ^DB_FILE_PERMISSIONS:660\nEMPLOYEE_TOTAL:5\nSUMMARY_TIMESTAMP:[0-9T:Z]{20}\n?$
name: task_000000_a7f5a24e
